Pharma · Power BI
Pharma · Healthcare · Power BI

Launch Analytics
Dashboard

A unified Power BI analytics suite consolidating HCP and DTC performance, website analytics, and partner data into a single source of truth, built for pharma commercial teams.

Status
Design Complete · Dev In Progress
My Role
End-to-End UI/UX: Research, Wireframing, PBI Layout Design, Dev Handoff
Platform
Power BI Dashboard Suite · Light Theme · Clinical UI
Tags
Power BI HCP DTC Multi-Channel Drill-Down Analytics

Research
02

03

05
PBI Handoff
The Brief in One Line

Pharma commercial teams were flying blind — five data sources, zero unified visibility, no way to act fast.

UX Challenge
Why This Was Hard to Design

The real challenge wasn't the data — it was the people. HCP and DTC teams had completely different mental models of what "performance" meant. Designing a single dashboard that served both without alienating either required deep alignment work before a single wireframe was drawn.

The Key UX Question We Kept Asking

"If a campaign director has 90 seconds in a boardroom, what is the one number they need to see — and what path do they take from there?"

Problem & Business Challenge
Five Core Pain Points
  • Campaign data fragmented across multiple vendor systems
  • Scattered metrics — no single unified dashboard view
  • No centralised performance tracking mechanism
  • Zero channel partner visibility or benchmarking
  • No way to compare performance across partners and vendors
Users & Stakeholders

Who This Was Designed For

  • Commercial Marketing Teams (HCP channel)
  • Consumer Marketing Teams (DTC channel)
  • Channel Partner Managers
  • Vendor Performance Analysts
  • Executive Leadership
Research & Key Insights

HCP & DTC worked in total silos

Completely separate reporting cadences made cross-channel comparison impossible without painful manual data merging each week.

Reports were backward-looking

Weekly static reports arrived 3–5 days late. Decisions were being made on outdated data, causing consistently missed campaign windows.

No drill-down capability existed

Managers saw headline numbers but had no path to identify which partner, vendor, or campaign was driving underperformance.

Wireframes
Design Approach & Information Architecture
Key Design Decisions

Critical Pivots

  • Split combined view into separate HCP / DTC dashboards after research revealed siloed team mental models
  • Drill-down hierarchy: Executive Summary → HCP / DTC → Performance Overview → Drivers → Deep Dive
  • PBI layout constraints applied from wireframe stage, eliminating developer rework
  • Light, clinical UI theme: white backgrounds, clean typography, clinical-red (#E8192C) as primary signal colour
  • HCP coded blue, DTC coded red, with distinct visual identities throughout the suite
Information Architecture
── Launch Overview (Executive Summary)
  ├── HCP Dashboard
  │  ├── Performance Overview
  │  ├── Performance Drivers
  │  ├── Channel / Partner Deep Dive
  │  └── Website Analytics
  └── DTC Dashboard
     ├── Performance Overview
     ├── Performance Drivers
     ├── Channel / Partner Deep Dive
     └── Website Analytics
Why We Did What We Did
  • Unified hierarchy, not tabs — We deliberately avoided a tab-per-channel model. Tabs would have reinforced the HCP/DTC silo mindset we were trying to break. A unified view with filter toggles forces users to think in terms of the full campaign, not just their channel.
  • Drill-down as the primary navigation model — Research showed that managers needed to go from "something is wrong" to "which vendor caused it" in under 3 clicks. Every screen level was designed around that path.
  • PBI layout constraints shaped the IA — Power BI has hard limits on canvas real estate. The information architecture was designed within those constraints first, then the visual design followed — not the other way around.
Design Decisions & Visual Identity
Colour Palette

Clinical Light Theme

Clinical Red #E8192CPrimary signal colour, HCP active states, key data points
Navy Blue #1A3A6BHCP channel identity, navigation accents, secondary actions
White #FFFFFFClean backgrounds, card surfaces, high readability
Light Grey #F2F4F6Section backgrounds, table rows, subtle dividers
Near-Black #1E1E1ETopbar navigation, primary text, header chrome
Typography & Visual Principles

Type System

  • Inter / System UI for body text and data labels
  • Bold weight for KPI values and metric figures
  • Regular weight for supporting labels and descriptions
  • Consistent 14px base size for dashboard readability

UI Principles

  • Light clinical theme: white-first, data-forward layout
  • HCP channel coded in blue, DTC coded in red throughout
  • Gauge charts for reach visualisation, bar charts for spend trends
  • Insight callouts surfaced at top of each view for immediate context
  • Consistent card-based component structure across all views
Final Design
Launch Analytics Dashboard · Marketing Analytics Report
HCP · Performance Overview
DTC · Performance Overview
Impact & Outcomes

Deliverables Produced
  • Journey maps for HCP and DTC stakeholder workflows
  • Wireframe V1 & V2 (PBI-compatible layout constraints)
  • Component specifications and design tokens
  • PBI-optimised dashboard layouts (all 5 hierarchy levels)
  • Developer handoff documentation with interaction specs
  • Design system foundations for the analytics suite
TraK360 Transformation Dashboard
Enterprise · C-Suite
Enterprise SaaS · C-Suite Analytics

TraK360
Transformation Dashboard

Executive-grade, real-time analytics for 100+ transformation initiatives across 14 business domains, replacing Excel and biweekly PowerPoints for a boardroom-level audience.

Status
Complete · Delivered
My Role
Lead Product Designer, End-to-End, Discovery to Handoff
Platform
Web Dashboard · Boardroom-Optimised · Dark Theme
Tags
C-Suite UX Real-Time Analytics Risk Visualisation Dark Luxury UI Portfolio Visibility
01
Discovery
02
Stakeholder Interviews
03
IA & Lo-Fi
04
Hi-Fi Screens
05
Design System
06
Handoff
The Brief in One Line

A multi-million pound transformation programme was being governed by spreadsheets. The C-suite had no real-time view of anything.

UX Challenge
Why This Was Hard to Design

The users were C-suite executives — people with almost zero tolerance for learning curves and absolute zero tolerance for ambiguity. Every interaction had to be immediately obvious. There was no room for onboarding flows or tooltips that explain what a chart means.

The Key UX Question We Kept Asking

"If the CTO opens this dashboard mid-board meeting, can they answer 'which initiatives are at risk and why' in under 3 minutes — without any prior training?"

Problem & Business Challenge
The Core Problem

Excel Managing a Portfolio Visibility

A massive transformation programme governed through spreadsheets and weekly PowerPoint decks. The C-suite had zero real-time visibility into value delivery, budget drift, or project risk. Every decision was made on stale, fragmented data.

Users & Stakeholders

Designing for the Boardroom

  • Chief Transformation Officer — primary user
  • CEO / C-Suite — strategic oversight & exceptions
  • Domain Leaders — category-level performance owners
  • Program Managers — operational tracking
The 4 Core Dashboard Views
View 01
Value Drivers
Home screen — top 10 value/cost projects + 6 KPI metrics. Value-first entry point for executives.
View 02
Budget / Timeline Drift
Cost vs schedule deviation with 3-tier risk thresholds: On Track / Potential Risk / At Risk (>30%).
View 03
Project Phase
Lifecycle across Ideate → Prepare → Deliver → Hypercare with drill-down detail table.
View 04
Domain View
Bubble chart — size = project count. First time 14 domains were compared simultaneously.
Wireframes
Design Decisions & Visual Identity
Key Design Decisions

Why Each Choice Was Made

  • Value-first hierarchy — most critical data visible without scrolling
  • Dual-mode budget drift toggle — schedule vs cost deviation views
  • Risk thresholds co-defined with finance — cross-stakeholder alignment
  • Bubble size encodes project count, not value — prevents visual distortion
  • Dark luxury aesthetic for boardroom, large-format display context
Visual Identity

Design Language — TraK360

Primary Pink — value signals & key accents
Gold — progress bars, Pink→Gold gradients
Deep Dark BG — boardroom optimised

Cormorant Garamond (display) · Syne (headers) · DM Sans (body)

Why We Did What We Did
  • Value-first, not alphabetical — The default view surfaces the top 10 projects by value/cost, not by name or date. The mental model of the CTO is always "what matters most right now" — not "give me a full list." We designed the entry point around that.
  • Co-defining risk thresholds with finance — The 3-tier risk language wasn't a design decision — it was a cross-stakeholder alignment exercise. We workshopped the thresholds with the finance team so the dashboard didn't just visualise risk, it became the shared language everyone used to discuss it.
  • Dark luxury aesthetic was a function, not a style choice — Boardroom presentations run on large-format displays in dim rooms. A light UI would have caused glare and washed out data. The dark theme was a considered decision based on the physical environment.
  • Bubble size = project count, not value — An early version used bubble size to represent financial value. In testing, executives kept misreading bubble area as relative importance. Switching to project count eliminated that confusion entirely.
Final Design
Impact & Outcomes
Real-Time
Full portfolio visible in real time
3 min
CTO insight time (was 2-hr weekly reviews)
14
Domains visualised comparatively for the first time
3-tier
Risk language standardised across all stakeholders
Deliverables Produced
  • Information architecture and navigation flow
  • Lo-fi wireframes for all 4 dashboard views
  • Hi-fi screens — complete screen set in dark luxury aesthetic
  • Developer handoff with interaction specs
Perfect Assortment — SKU Planner
CPG · Global Retail
CPG · Global Retail · SKU Optimisation

Perfect Assortment —
SKU Planner

A centralised web application that replaced manual Excel SKU workflows across global markets — automating prioritisation, applying constraints, and delivering standardised Must-Have / Next-Best outputs.

Status
Complete · Delivered to Dev
My Role
Product Designer — Requirements, UX Structure, Wireframing, UI Design, Dev Delivery
Platform
Web Application · Global Multi-Market
Tags
Web App SKU Optimisation Constraint-Based Global Rollout CPG
01
Requirement Gathering
02
Lo-Fi Wireframes
03
Hi-Fi UI Design
04
Delivery & Handoff
The Brief in One Line

Every market was doing SKU planning in their own Excel — no consistency, no automation, no way to compare results globally.

UX Challenge
Why This Was Hard to Design

The users were analysts who had built years of personal workflows in Excel. The product had to feel faster and more powerful than what they already knew — or they'd simply go back to their spreadsheets. The biggest UX risk wasn't poor usability. It was building something technically correct that people refused to adopt.

The Key UX Question We Kept Asking

"Does this feel like it was designed for the analyst sitting at this screen for 6 hours a day, or does it feel like it was designed by someone who's never done SKU planning?"

Problem & Business Challenge
The Core Problem

Excel, Market by Market

  • Each market used its own Excel tracker — zero standardisation
  • No consistent constraints applicable across markets
  • SKU prioritisation slow, manual, and error-prone
  • Must-Have / Next-Best lists required full analyst effort to generate
  • No cross-market performance visibility into SKU coverage
Users & Personas

Who Uses This Platform

  • Assortment Planning Analysts — day-to-day workflow users
  • Category Managers — constraint setters & output reviewers
  • Market Leads — regional performance oversight
  • Global Planning Teams — cross-market consistency guardians
Core Platform Features
Feature 01
SKU Prioritisation Engine
Automated ranking based on configurable criteria. Replaces manual Excel scoring entirely across all markets.
Feature 02
Constraint Builder
Apply distribution limits, margin floors, and market-specific rules inline without leaving the planning view.
Feature 03
Classification Insights
Persistent SKU performance signals always visible in a sidebar throughout the entire planning flow.
Feature 04
Standardised Export
Must-Have / Next-Best outputs exported in a consistent format, applicable uniformly across all global markets.
Wireframes
Design Approach
UX Decisions That Matter
  • Single integrated workspace — no multi-step wizard, no context-switching between screens
  • Inline constraint editing while the full planning view stays visible
  • Persistent classification sidebar throughout — constant signal visibility
  • Standardised export templates ensure market-independent output consistency
Design Principles Applied
  • Reduce cognitive load — everything the planner needs on one screen
  • Consistency as a feature — same interface logic across all global markets
  • Trust through transparency — always show why a SKU is ranked where it is
  • Progressive complexity — constraints optional, not mandatory, for new users
Why We Did What We Did
  • Single integrated workspace, no wizard — Early explorations used a step-by-step wizard: define constraints → run prioritisation → review outputs. Users hated it. They needed to see the constraint editor and the SKU list simultaneously because changing a constraint and immediately seeing what flips in the ranking is the entire job. Collapsing that into a single view was the breakthrough.
  • Persistent classification sidebar — Analysts needed to know why a SKU ranked where it did, not just where it ranked. The sidebar showing performance signals was designed to never disappear — it anchors every decision in evidence, which also builds trust in the output.
  • Consistency as the real product — The most important design decision was standardising the export format. On the surface that sounds like a technical detail. But for global planning teams, the ability to compare a market in Brazil with one in Germany on the same template was the actual value delivered. The UI was the tool. The standardised output was the product.
Final Design
Impact & Outcomes
↓
Manual reporting effort significantly reduced
⚡
Faster SKU prioritisation across all markets
≡
Cross-market consistency finally achieved
✦
Standardised Must-Have / Next-Best outputs
Deliverables Produced
  • User personas and detailed problem definition documentation
  • Use case mapping and feature exploration documentation
  • Low-fidelity wireframes — complete end-to-end user flow
  • High-fidelity UI designs — full screen set for all core flows
  • Developer handoff documentation with component specs
  • Interaction annotations and state specifications

About

From
Data
to Design.

1
Origin
Data Analytics
Forecasting models and analytics systems across CPG and retail. Built genuine fluency in how data behaves at scale.
2
The Pivot
Analytics → Design
Recognised that generating insight was only half the problem. Communicating it clearly was the harder challenge.
3
Now
Product Designer
Specialising in analytics platforms and data-driven SaaS. End-to-end ownership — research to developer handoff.

Complex data shouldn't require a PhD to interpret. The designer's job is to make the right decision obvious.

I specialise in the intersection of analytics systems and human decision-making. My foundation in data gives me fluency with the underlying logic of dashboards, KPI hierarchies, and reporting pipelines — meaning I design with the data, not just around it.

In 2.8 years of focused product design practice, I've worked across Healthcare & Pharma, CPG, Retail, Enterprise SaaS, and Financial Platforms — consistently solving one core challenge: how do you surface the right signal from noisy, high-stakes data so the right person can act in seconds, not hours?

Design Philosophy

How I Think.

01
Data-First Product Thinking

Every project starts by understanding the data model — what signals exist, what's the grain, what the data can and can't say. Beautiful dashboards that surface the wrong answers are worse than no dashboard at all.

02
Designing for Decisions, Not Views

A dashboard that shows data isn't the goal. The goal is a decision made with confidence. Every layout choice is evaluated against: does this make the right action obvious?

03
Clear Information Hierarchy

I design strict hierarchies — what lives above the fold, what is a drill-down, what is an exception alert. Senior stakeholders should never scroll to find the signal.

04
Simplifying Complex Systems

My analytics background lets me decompose complex data architectures into mental models that match how analysts and executives actually think — not just how the data is stored.

05
Collaboration Across Functions

The best analytics products are built in close partnership with data engineers, PMs, and business stakeholders. I facilitate metric alignment before a wireframe is ever drawn.

06
Business Impact as North Star

Every design decision should trace to a business outcome — reduced reporting effort, faster decisions, better spend efficiency. I design with measurable impact in mind from day one.
